coredata.dc:title: A theoretical study of 0D Ti2CO2/2D g-C3N4 Schottky-junction for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Yazhuo
Wang, Erpeng
Zhou, Jian
Sun, Zhimei
coredata.dc:description: The construction of Schottky junctions is considered to be an efficient way to boost spatial charge separation and transfer in photocatalytic systems. In this work, we construct the Schottky junction using zero-dimensional (0D) Ti2CO2 nanoclusters (TCO) and two-dimensional (2D) g-C3N4 (GCN), denoted as TCO/GCN. The interfacial interaction, separation of photogenerated carriers, and reaction driving force of the TCO/GCN have been investigated based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations. TCO can serve as an electron acceptor due to the larger work function relative to the semiconductor GCN. The band bending caused by the interfacial charge prevents the electrons from flowing back to the semiconductor, which can effectively separate the photo-excited carriers. The metal sites exposed at the edge of TCO exhibit high hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity. This study provides a new idea for the development of 0D/2D Schottky junction photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: First-principles calculation
MXenes
Schottky junction
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFB3807200). The authors also thank the high-performance computing (HPC) resources of Beihang University.
This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFB3807200 ). The authors also thank the high-performance computing (HPC) resources of Beihang University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Structure of g-C3N4 monolayer (top view). The red dashed lines show the unit cell; the gray and blue balls stand for C and N atoms, respectively. (b) The band structure and PDOS, and (c) electrostatic potential of g-C3N4 monolayer. The red and blue dashed lines are the Fermi level and the vacuum level, respectively. The Fermi level is set to zero. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Optimized TCO structures of (a) Ti(O)-centered type A, (b) C-centered type A, (c) Ti(O)-centered type B, (d) C-centered type B.

The PDOS of TCO (left side) and the charge density distribution of defect states marked with the yellow circle on the PDOS (right side). The Fermi level is set to zero. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The combined structure of TCO and GCN with (a) 1/6 concentration (one TCO attached to 2 × 3 × 1 supercell of GCN) and (b) 1/15 concentration (one TCO attached to 3 × 5 × 1 supercell of GCN). The electrostatic potential of (c) 1/6 concentration and (d) 1/15 concentration. The red and blue dashed lines are the Fermi level and the vacuum level, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The 3D charge density difference for TCO/GCN with an isovalue of 0.0002 e Å−3 from the side view (left side) and top view (right side). (b) Planar-averaged charge density difference Δρ(z) for TCO/GCN. The pink and blue regions represent the charge accumulation and depletion in the space, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Hirshfeld charges on freestanding TCO (left); TCO and GCN components in the TCO/GCN junction (middle); freestanding GCN (right). The negative and positive values represent the negative and positive charges carried in the corresponding component, respectively.

Diagram of the energy positions before(a) and after(b) contact of TCO and GCN, and the mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction of TCO/GCN Schottky-junction. The gray solid lines stand for the conduction bands (CB) and valence bands (VB) of GCN. The Fermi level of TCO is indicated by the dashed lines.

The free energy steps for hydrogen reduction at site-1 with (a) pH = 0, (b) pH = 2; site-2 with (c) pH = 0, (d) pH = 2; site-3 with (e) pH = 0, (f) pH = 2. Ue is the potential provided by photogenerated electrons.

head.ce:title: A theoretical study of 0D Ti2CO2/2D g-C3N4 Schottky-junction for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of Schottky junctions is considered to be an efficient way to boost spatial charge separation and transfer in photocatalytic systems. In this work, we construct the Schottky junction using zero-dimensional (0D) Ti2CO2 nanoclusters (TCO) and two-dimensional (2D) g-C3N4 (GCN), denoted as TCO/GCN. The interfacial interaction, separation of photogenerated carriers, and reaction driving force of the TCO/GCN have been investigated based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations. TCO can serve as an electron acceptor due to the larger work function relative to the semiconductor GCN. The band bending caused by the interfacial charge prevents the electrons from flowing back to the semiconductor, which can effectively separate the photo-excited carriers. The metal sites exposed at the edge of TCO exhibit high hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity. This study provides a new idea for the development of 0D/2D Schottky junction photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in water driven by solar energy is a green and cost-effective solution to the growing energy demand and associated environmental problems[1–4]. Over the past decades, various semiconductor materials have been successfully used as photocatalysts in HER, such as metal oxides[5], nitrides[6,7], black phosphorus[8,9], transition metal dichalcogenides (TMD)[10], transition metal carbides/carbonitrides/nitrides (MXenes)[11,12], and perovskites[13]. Among them, two-dimensional (2D) materials have attracted great interest due to their unique physical properties, remarkable optical and electrical properties.

Since Wang et al. reported that the metal-free 2D g-C3N4 (named GCN) can generate hydrogen from water under visible light irradiation[14], GCN has rapidly developed in the field of photocatalysis, due to its good visible-light response, unique geometric and electronic structure, and high chemical and thermal stabilities[6,15–27]. However, owing to its π-conjugated electronic structure, GCN has a weak dipole field and exhibits fast carrier recombination[28–30], which limits its application in the field of photocatalytic HER. Constructing a Schottky-junction in photocatalytic systems can promote the separation and transfer of photo-excited charges and prevent their flow-back to the semiconductor[24,31–35], thus effectively reducing the probability of recombination. Even though single-atom catalysts of noble metals can play a positive role for this purpose, easy aggregation and high costs cause great difficulty in practical application[6,36,37]. There is an urgent need to find feasible and economical alternatives to noble metals.

As a newly developed family of 2D materials, 2D transition metal carbides/carbonitrides/nitrides (MXenes) have attracted widespread attention since Ti3C2 was discovered in 2011[38]. Because of their hydrophilicity, large specific surface area, high electrical conductivity and compositional variability, MXenes have been intensively studied and applied, especially in photocatalysts[11,12,39–42]. In recent years, there have been several reports of 2D Ti2C being used as a co-catalyst for photocatalysis in photocatalytic processes[43,44]. Compared to naked 2D Ti2C, the O-terminated zero-dimensional (0D) Ti2CO2 (named TCO) with smaller size has superior stability than naked 2D Ti2C[45,46] and is easier to graft onto a variety of semiconductors to form heterostructures[40,47,48]. Moreover, the abundant exposed metal sites on the clusters exhibit strong redox ability, which has a positive role in the catalytic reactions[40,49].

In this work, we built a 0D Ti2CO2/2D g-C3N4 (TCO/GCN) Schottky junction and performed periodic DFT calculations to investigate its structural and electronic properties. Notably, the charge distribution in TCO/GCN was analyzed through the charge density difference and Hirshfeld charge. The formation mechanism of the Schottky barrier which can effectively separate carriers and promote photocatalytic reaction was also analyzed. Finally, the free energy step diagrams for HER were also calculated to investigate the driving forces of the reactions.

Our DFT calculations were performed by using the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) with the projector augment wave (PAW) method[50,51]. The exchange and correlation potential were described with the Perdew − Burke − Ernzerhof (PBE) version of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA)[52]. The DFT-D3 method[53,54] was employed to describe the interlayer vdW interactions. The cutoff energy for electronic wave functions was set to 550 eV. A vacuum layer larger than 25 Å was set to avoid any artificial interaction between periodically repeated systems. Since our system is large enough, the two-dimensional Brillouin zone was sampled with Gamma-only and 2 × 2 × 1 k-point grids for geometry optimization and self-consistent calculations, respectively. The Heyd–Scuseria–Ernzerhof (HSE06) hybrid functional[55] was adopted for monolayer GCN to obtain accurate electronic structures. For TCO/GCN nanocomposite, we adopt the DFT + U method to save computational resources. The U value of 7.2 eV was applied for the Ti-3d electrons in the TCO/GCN. The band gap of 2D monolayer Ti2CO2 calculated with this U value is in good agreement with that calculated by HSE06.

Before studying the TCO/GCN nanocomposite, we first investigated the structures and electronic properties of the freestanding GCN and TCO, respectively. For GCN, the optimized lattice parameters are a = b = 4.78 Å, as depicted in Fig. 1 (a). These results are in good agreement with previously reported data[56]. Two kinds of N atoms are present in 2D GCN, NI and NII, which have different surroundinig coordination atomic environments. The NI atom is fully saturated by the surrounding C atoms, while the NII atom is bound to two C atoms, leaving a dangling bond.

The GCN has a direct band gap with conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) situated at Γ point, as can be evinced from Fig. 1(b). The calculated band gap value was 3.16 eV, in agreement with previous theoretical and experimental reports[57,58]. From the projected density of state (PDOS), the VBM can be observed to be dominated by the NII-2p orbitals and the CBM is dominated by the NII-2p and C-2p orbitals. On the other hand, the NI atoms make a slight contribution to the VBM and CBM compared to the NII atoms, which confirms that the two kinds of N atoms are in different atomic environments. Besides, based on the value of the electrostatic potential in Fig. 1(c), the work function of 2D GCN was calculated as 4.2 eV. Herein, the work function is defined as
ϕ =
E
v a c
-
E F
, where E vac is the energy of a stationary electron in vacuum and EF is the Fermi level of the material.

For TCO, its 2D basic structure and electronic structure are shown in Fig. S1. The Ti2CO2 nanosheet has a hexagonal-like lattice (top view) and a sandwich configuration (side view). The optimized lattice parameters are a = b = 3.02 Å, which are consistent with literature data[12,59]. From its band structure, it can be seen that 2D Ti2CO2 is an indirect bandgap semiconductor with a gap of 1.0 eV. However, its conduction band minimum (CBM) does not cross the reduction potential of H+/H2 thus it is unable to be applied in photocatalytic HER alone. These are consistent with previous results[12,60]. However, the modification of 2D materials to 0D ones may lead to larger band gaps due to quantum confinement effect or introduce defect states due to edge state effect [41,61–64]. The electronic structure of the parent 2D materials can thus be regulated. Therefore, we attempted to modify 2D Ti2CO2 into 0D configuration to investigate its electronic properties and the possibility of combining it with GCN for photocatalytic HER.

As far as we know, 0D MXenes tend to form hexagonal shapes[41,65], so we constructed two types of hexagons with a lateral size of about 1.1 nm (see Fig. S2): type A hexagons (Fig. S2(a),(b)), which are regular hexagons with smooth edges and stepped edges, and type B hexagons (Fig. S2(c),(d)), in which the vertex angle atoms are cut. Each of these two hexagon types is further divided into Ti(O)-centered (Fig. S2(a),(c)) and C-centered (Fig. S2(b),(d)) hexagons. By implementing this classification and construction, all possible hexagonal TCO species can theoretically be traversed. However, all the structures exhibit some deformations after geometrical optimization, as can be evinced from Fig. 2 . These deformations mainly occurred on the oxygen atoms at the edges, which are stretched outwardly significantly as compared to the initial structure. The most significant change is observed in C-centered type B hexagons (Fig. 2(d)), whose morphology switches from truncated hexagons to regular hexagons as a result of the outspread of the oxygen atoms.

In order to further confirm the stability of TCO, an ab initio molecular dynamics simulation (AIMD)[66] was performed. After 6 ps of simulation at 300 K, the C-centered type A structure failed to remain stable, while the rest of the structures retained their original shapes. As can be evinced from Fig. S3, at 300 K for 6 ps, the values of the total energy of the structures fluctuate slightly around the average value. After the simulation, no broken bonds or obvious geometric reconstructions were identified in the structure diagrams, suggesting that they are thermodynamically stable at room temperature. The normalized pair correlation function g(r) verifies that the structure can keep good crystallinity after the annealing simulation.

We then calculated the PDOS of several stable TCO structures selected from above, namely, Ti-centered type A, Ti-centered type B, and C-centered type B. As shown in Fig. S4, the electronic states of these structures are continuously distributed at the Fermi energy level, indicating that all these structures are metallic. Therefore, we refer to them as nanoclusters in the remaining part of the paper. Metallic materials can form Schottky junctions with semiconductors and act as electronic sinks in photocatalytic reactions to facilitate carrier separation[3,25,67]. The necessary prerequisite for the formation of Schottky junctions is that the work function of the metal is larger than that of the semiconductor[24,68]. In this work, the work functions of the Ti(O)-centered type A, Ti(O)-centered type B and C-centered type B nanoclusters were calculated as 3.92 eV, 3.84 eV, and 4.74 eV, respectively; notably, the nanocluster with a value for the work function larger than that of GCN (4.2 eV) can theoretically form Schottky contacts with it.

Considering the above-described requirements for the work function we selected the C-centered type B TCO nanocluster to combine with GCN to form Schottky junction. We first investigated the electronic properties of the C-centered type B TCO nanocluster, as can be evinced in Fig. 3 . The PDOS indicates that the electronic states are continuously distributed at the Fermi level and exhibit a high electronic density. However, a sub-gap is present in the occupied state below the Fermi level, which implies the introduction of defective states. To further understand these defective states, we calculated the orbital charge density distribution at the edge of the sub-gap, which is shown on the right side of Fig. 3. Based on the electron cloud density, it can be seen that the defective states are uniformly distributed around Ti and O, which implies the formation of clusters caused a rearrangement of the charges around Ti and O, thereby introducing defective energy levels.

Subsequently, we discussed the properties of TCO/GCN by varying the concentration of TCO nanoclusters attached to the two-dimensional GCN surface. Here, the concentration of TCO nanoclusters in the TCO/GCN was adjusted by attaching TCO nanoclusters to the two-dimensional GCN surface of 2 × 3 × 1 and 3 × 5 × 1, respectively, denoting as 1/6 and 1/15. Fig. 4 presents the structures and electrostatic potentials of these two heterojunctions characterized by different nanocluster concentrations. The heterojunction with a lower nanocluster concentration (1/15) has a lower value for the work function (corresponding to a higher value for the Fermi energy) than the heterojunction with a higher nanocluster concentration (1/6), as shown in Fig. 4(c) (d). Generally, in order for the system to facilitate photocatalytic HER, the Fermi level needs to be higher than the reduction potential of H+/H2 (−4.44 eV vs Vacuum)[69,70], thus the structure with a 1/15 nanocluster concentration was selected here.

The PDOS depicted in Fig. S5 describes the contributions of the TCO and GCN components to the combined structure. We can see that TCO is metallic and it exhibits a continuous energy distribution around the Fermi level. The value for the band gap of the GCN component is 1.9 eV, which is much smaller than the corresponding parameter for isolated GCN. This difference may be attributed to the interaction between GCN and TCO, which deforms the structure and unit cell of the former[71–73]. To explore the bonding mechanism and charge transfer process, the charge density difference (CDD) was calculated via the following equation[74]:
Δ ρ =
ρ
TCO / G C N
-
ρ
TCO
-
ρ
GCN

where ρTCO/GCN, ρTCO, and ρGCN represent the charge density of the TCO/GCN heterojunction, TCO and GCN, respectively. Based on Fig. 5 (a), it can be inferred that charge is redistributed near the interface area, that is, it accumulates close to the TCO layer and is depleted near the GCN layer. The planar-averaged CDD along the Z direction also exhibited charge transfer and rearrangement as illustrated in Fig. 5(b). The change of charge at the interface demonstrates that the electron transfer occurs only at the interface, mainly from the GCN layer to the TCO across the interface, whereas almost no change in charge density is observed in the inner section of TCO.

We also quantitatively analyzed the specifics of the charge transfer. Herein we chose the more advantageous Hirshfeld charge analysis over the Mulliken and Bader charge analysis. Because the former implements spatial integration over an atomic domain to get rid of basis set dependency, which provides a meaningful charge value and conforms to chemical experience[75]. As can be evinced from Fig. 6 , the TCO component in the combination has a more negative charge than freestanding TCO, while the GCN component has a more positive charge than freestanding GCN. These trends indicate that TCO has a higher negative charge affinity than GCN, so electrons tend to migrate toward TCO when GCN and TCO are combined.

A schematic diagram of the electron transfer and the photocatalytic mechanism is depicted in Fig. 7 . Under light irradiation, photo-excited charges will be generated in the GCN section of the heterojunction. The electrons will be excited to the CB of the GCN, leaving photo-excited holes at VB. TCO with a larger work function (a lower Fermi level) than the semiconductor GCN serves as an electron acceptor. Then, the electron will migrate from GCN toward TCO until their Fermi levels are aligned. At that time, the position of the Fermi level of the system straddles the H+/H potential, so the electrons accumulated on the TCO side of the heterojunction will participate in the reduction reaction to produce hydrogen. While the holes left at the VBM will be consumed and participate in the oxidation reaction. The formation of the Schottky barrier induced by the presence of TCO will trap electrons and inhibit the migration of surface-accumulated electrons back to the semiconductor and recombine with holes.

In order to trigger the hydrogen reduction reaction, the photogenerated carriers need to provide a sufficient driving force for the process to take place, that is, the sufficient external potential. Hence, we specifically discuss the process and mechanisms of the HER and calculate the energies required for the reactions. The standard reduction potential changes with the pH in accordance with the following equation:
E
H
+
/
H 2
red
= - 4.44 + 0.059 × p H
[76–78]. According to previously published studies, the external potential Ue supplied by photogenerated electrons for HER is defined as the energy difference between the CBM and the reduction potential of H+/H2:
U e
= C B M -
( - 4.44 + 0.059 × P H )
[78–80]. For the system we studied, the CBM is located above the H+/H2 potential only at pH ≤ 2. In other words, the photogenerated electrons can provide the driving force for the reaction under the aforementioned pH conditions. Therefore, in the following section of the paper, we focus on the two cases of pH = 0 and pH = 2.

The HER involves only two steps, as depicted in Fig. 8 . First, a proton from the solution forms the adsorbed H* species; next, a H2 molecule is generated after the H* species reacts with a proton and an electron. The adsorption energy of hydrogen atom was evaluated on Ti and O atoms in different positions. The results show that the adsorption energy at Ti atoms are significantly lower than that at O atoms. Therefore we only discuss the HER at all possible Ti sites, as shown in Fig.S6, where site-1 and site-3 correspond to Ti atoms located on the edges of TCO, and site-2 is on the surface above TCO.

As can be clearly evinced from Fig. 8, the values for the Gibbs free energy difference (ΔGH*) of the reactions at these sites are all negative at pH = 0, indicating that the reactions could proceed spontaneously. For site-1, the absolute value of ΔGH* (|ΔGH*|) is very low, so the desorption process of the product is thermodynamically unfavorable when pH = 0 and at pH = 2. By contrast, site-2 exhibits better performance than site-1, especially at pH = 2. For this site, the value of |ΔGH*| is close to the catalytic threshold of 0.2 eV[80–82], exhibiting good HER performance. From a thermodynamic standpoint, site-3 has the best HER performance among all adsorption sites: its |ΔGH*| is lower than the catalytic threshold under both pH environments and with or without light irradiation. Thus, the adsorption and desorption can proceed efficiently, implying a high HER activity.

We have investigated the structural, electronic, and photocatalytic properties of TCO, GCN, and their composites based on DFT calculations. The various TCO species constructed all exhibit metallic properties. As a result of the difference between the work functions of TCO and GCN, when two components combine, the photo-excited electrons would be injected into TCO from GCN until their Fermi energy levels reach equilibrium. The band bending caused by the interfacial charge prevents the electrons from flowing back to the semiconductor, which can efficiently separate the photo-excited carriers. Furthermore, the active sites exposed at the edges of the clusters have good HER catalytic properties. The findings of this study pave the way for the design of novel heterojunctions on providing efficient photocatalytic reactions.

Yazhuo Zheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Erpeng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Jian Zhou: Resources, Supervision, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Zhimei Sun: Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFB3807200). The authors also thank the high-performance computing (HPC) resources of Beihang University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.156562.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor polymeric graphitic carbon nitride photocatalysts: the “holy grail” for the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction under visible light
